sanskrit_devanagari:
न चैतद्विद्मः कतरन्नो गरीयो
यद्वा जयेम यदि वा नो जयेयुः ।
यानेव हत्वा न जिजीविषामस्
तेऽवस्थिताः प्रमुखे धार्तराष्ट्राः ॥ ६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
na caitad vidmaḥ kataran no garīyo
yad vā jayema yadi vā no jayeyuḥ |
yān eva hatvā na jijīviṣāmas
te 'vasthitāḥ pramukhe dhārtarāṣṭrāḥ || 6 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.006 = MBh_06,024.006). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: Nor do we know which is better — conquering them or being conquered by them. If we killed the sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra, we should not care to live. Yet they are now standing before us on the battlefield.

We do not even know which result of this war is preferable for us—conquering them or being conquered by them. Even after killing them we will not desire to live. Yet they have taken the side of the sons of Dhritarasthra, and now stand before us on the battlefield.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Arjuna did not know whether he should fight and risk unnecessary violence, although fighting is the duty of the kṣatriyas, or whether he should refrain and live by begging. If he did not conquer the enemy, begging would be his only means of subsistence.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.copyright_holder: Advaita Ashrama, Kolkata (publisher of Swami Gambhirananda's English translation of Sankara-bhasya). No verbatim is captured because no commentary exists for this verse.
commentaries.notes: Site bhagavad-gita.us states explicitly that Sri Sankaracharya did not comment on this sloka and that his commentary starts from 2.10. Standard Advaita Ashrama (Gambhirananda) edition agrees - Sankara's bhasya begins at BG 2.10.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 'na caitad vidmaḥ' — 'we do not know' — as the explicit confession of confusion that prepares the ground for Arjuna's surrender in 2.7. The verse is the bottom of the descent into not-knowing; the next verse is the turn upward into discipleship.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.6 – 2.8 If you say, ‘After beginning the war, if we withdraw from the battle, the sons of Dhrtarastra will slay us all forcibly’, be it so. I think that even to be killed by them, who do not know the difference between righteousness and unrighteousness, is better for us than gaining unrighteous vi
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja emphasises that the not-knowing here is genuine — Arjuna is not posturing. Both outcomes (victory and defeat) appear equally undesirable from inside his current frame. The verse is therefore evidence that the tactical question cannot be answered tactically; the chapter must reframe.

positions.rendering: which is better — conquering them or being conquered
which result is preferable for us
which of the two is weightier / more important

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: garīyaḥ literally means 'heavier / weightier' (cognate with English 'grave'). Translators flatten it to 'better' or 'preferable', which is reasonable. The literal sense is more interesting: Arjuna is not asking which is morally better, he is asking which carries more weight. The two outcomes feel equally consequential and equally awful, which is precisely the symptom of the framing being wrong.

literal_meaning: And we do not know which of the two is weightier for us — whether we should conquer them or whether they should conquer us. Those whom, having killed, we would not wish to live, those very sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra are positioned before us in battle.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Arjuna's third move: explicit confession of not-knowing. He no longer claims the moral high ground; he reports that both outcomes appear equally undesirable from where he is standing. The commentarial tradition treats this as the bottom of the descent — the moment Arjuna runs out of arguments and admits that his current frame cannot generate an answer. The verse functions as the immediate setup for 2.7, where Arjuna will explicitly surrender to the teaching.

remaining_gaps: Shankara verbatim — site states he did not comment on 2.6; bhasya begins at 2.10.
